101.title: Variables & Data Types
101.content:
Introduction to Variables

In JavaScript, variables are containers for storing data values. JavaScript uses let, const, and var keywords to declare variables.

Use const when you know the value won't change, and let when it might. Avoid var in modern JavaScript due to scoping issues.

Data Types

JavaScript has 7 primitive data types: String, Number, Boolean, Undefined, Null, Symbol, and BigInt. Objects are the only non-primitive type.

102.title: Functions & Scope
102.content:
Defining Functions

Functions are reusable blocks of code. In JavaScript you can define them as function declarations, function expressions, or arrow functions.

Arrow functions (const fn = () => {}) are concise and don't bind their own this, making them ideal for callbacks.

Scope

Scope determines where variables are accessible. Variables declared with let or const are block-scoped. Understanding global, function, and block scope helps you avoid subtle bugs.

103.title: DOM Manipulation
103.content:
The Document Object Model

The DOM is a programming interface for HTML documents. It represents the page so programs can change structure, style, and content dynamically.

Use document.querySelector() to select elements and addEventListener() to respond to user interactions like clicks and key presses.

104.title: Arrays & Objects
104.content:
Working with Arrays

Arrays store ordered collections of data. Master methods like map(), filter(), reduce(), and forEach() to manipulate data declaratively without loops.

Objects

Objects store key-value pairs. Destructuring, spread operators, and object methods let you write cleaner, more expressive code.

105.title: Async JavaScript
105.content:
Asynchronous Programming

JavaScript is single-threaded but handles async operations via callbacks, Promises, and async/await. Understanding the event loop is essential for writing non-blocking code.

async/await syntax makes asynchronous code read like synchronous code, drastically improving readability and error handling.

106.title: ES6+ Features
106.content:
Modern JavaScript

ES6 introduced template literals, destructuring, default parameters, rest/spread operators, modules, and class syntax. These features are now standard in all modern JavaScript projects.

201.title: Flexbox Fundamentals
201.content:
CSS Flexbox

Flexbox is a one-dimensional layout method for laying out items in rows or columns. It distributes space along the main axis and aligns items on the cross axis.

Key properties: display: flex, justify-content, align-items, flex-direction, flex-wrap.

202.title: CSS Grid Deep Dive
202.content:
CSS Grid Layout

Grid is a two-dimensional layout system. Define rows and columns with grid-template-columns and grid-template-rows. Place items with grid-column and grid-row.

The fr unit distributes available space proportionally — it's the core of responsive grid design.

203.title: Responsive Breakpoints
203.content:
Media Queries

Media queries let you apply CSS rules based on device characteristics like screen width. Mobile-first approach: start with mobile styles, then add complexity for larger screens using min-width breakpoints.

204.title: Advanced Layout Patterns
204.content:
Real-world Layouts

Combine Grid and Flexbox for complex layouts: use Grid for page structure and Flexbox for component alignment. Holy grail layout, card grids, sidebar layouts — all become simple with these two tools.

301.title: Python Basics
301.content:
Python Fundamentals

Python's clean syntax makes it ideal for data work. Core concepts: variables, loops, conditionals, functions, and list comprehensions.

302.title: Pandas DataFrames
302.content:
Working with Pandas

Pandas provides DataFrame and Series structures for tabular data. Load CSVs with pd.read_csv(), then filter, group, merge, and transform data with expressive, readable syntax.

303.title: Data Visualization
303.content:
Matplotlib & Seaborn

Visualize data with line charts, bar charts, scatter plots, and heatmaps. Matplotlib is foundational; Seaborn provides high-level, beautiful statistical visualizations built on top of it.

304.title: NumPy Arrays
304.content:
Numerical Computing

NumPy's ndarray enables fast vectorized operations on large datasets without Python loops. Master slicing, broadcasting, and linear algebra basics.

305.title: Intro to Machine Learning
305.content:
ML Concepts

Supervised learning uses labeled data to train models. Scikit-learn provides clean APIs: fit(), predict(), score(). Start with linear regression and decision trees before moving to ensemble methods.

401.title: React Basics & JSX
401.content:
What is React?

React is a JavaScript library for building user interfaces using a component-based architecture. JSX lets you write HTML-like syntax in JavaScript files, which Babel compiles to React.createElement calls at build time.

402.title: useState & useEffect
402.content:
React Hooks

useState lets you add state to function components. useEffect handles side effects like data fetching and subscriptions. Together they replace class component lifecycle methods completely.

403.title: Component Patterns
403.content:
Reusable Components

Props pass data down; callback functions pass events up. Controlled components bind form values to state. Lifting state up, compound components, and render props are patterns every React developer must know.

404.title: React Router
404.content:
Client-side Routing

React Router enables navigation without full page reloads. Define routes with <Route>, navigate with <Link>, and read URL parameters with useParams().

405.title: Context API
405.content:
Global State

Context avoids prop drilling by making state available anywhere in the component tree. Create a context, wrap your app in a Provider, and consume it with useContext().

406.title: Performance & Optimization
406.content:
React Performance

React.memo, useMemo, and useCallback prevent unnecessary re-renders. Code splitting with React.lazy and Suspense reduces bundle size for faster initial loads.

501.title: Docker Containers
501.content:
Containerization

Containers package code and its dependencies together, ensuring consistent environments across development and production. A Dockerfile defines the environment; docker build creates the image; docker run starts a container.

502.title: Docker Compose
502.content:
Multi-container Apps

Docker Compose orchestrates multiple containers with a YAML file. Define services, networks, and volumes. One docker-compose up command starts your entire application stack.

503.title: Kubernetes Overview
503.content:
K8s Architecture

Kubernetes orchestrates container deployment, scaling, and management. Core concepts: pods, deployments, services, and namespaces. The control plane manages worker nodes automatically.

504.title: Deployments & Services
504.content:
Deploying with K8s

A Deployment manages a ReplicaSet to ensure the desired number of pods are running. A Service exposes pods to network traffic — ClusterIP, NodePort, and LoadBalancer serve different use cases.

601.title: Design Principles
601.content:
Core Design Principles

Proximity, alignment, repetition, and contrast (PARC) form the foundation of visual design. Understanding these principles helps you evaluate and improve any interface systematically.

602.title: Color Theory
602.content:
Using Color Effectively

Color conveys meaning, creates hierarchy, and evokes emotion. Learn about hue, saturation, and lightness. Master complementary, analogous, and triadic color schemes for balanced, accessible palettes.

603.title: Typography
603.content:
Type as Design Material

Typography is more than font choice. Line height, letter spacing, font weight, and scale all affect readability and hierarchy. Pair a display face with a workhorse body face for strong contrast.

604.title: Wireframing
604.content:
From Idea to Structure

Wireframes communicate layout and functionality without visual design decisions. Low-fidelity wireframes iterate fast; high-fidelity ones communicate detail to engineers and stakeholders.

605.title: Prototyping in Figma
605.content:
Interactive Prototypes

Figma's prototyping feature links frames with transitions to simulate real app interactions. User testing with prototypes catches usability issues before a single line of code is written.
